title: A Visual Proof That One Layer Can Compute Anything
tagline: Flatten sigmoids into steps, glue bumps, stack towers — Nielsen's visual proof of universality, made interactive
summary: Rebuild Michael Nielsen's visual proof of the Universal Approximation Theorem by hand: crank a sigmoid into a step, subtract two steps into a bump, design a 1D function with a bank of height sliders (and meet the σ⁻¹ subtlety), then stack thresholded towers to fit a 2D surface.
highlights: Watch a sigmoid snap into a step function and learn Nielsen's reparametrization by step position s = -b/w.
Subtract two steps to make a bump, then design a whole function with a bank of per-bump height sliders — including the σ⁻¹∘f twist the output sigmoid hides.
Build a 2D tower from four sigmoid steps and tile towers across the plane to approximate any surface, watching the fit sharpen with resolution.
